item.title: Great Read
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely Fantastic, take my +1
I greatly enjoyed this read. It was a nice take on the government using superpowered individuals to fight a war.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, I did not know that word, though I am Japanese.
Can't believe i've never read this. This was fantastic, lots of juicy little breadcrumbs and a dank overall story. Take my +200 and a pat on the back. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A really interesting approach to the history.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shinka could also be 神化.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was somehow deeply touched by the word "Pingfang" since I was born in the city where Unit 731 was located.
I know you not just used Unit 731 as the background but indeed did some research about it.
It reminded me of my hometown and some horrifying stories about the Anti-Japanese War and Unit 731 that my grandma told.

I notice that some people thought it was not proper to use Unit 731 in a fictional story. However, many Chinese complain that western media usually ignore the great loss of China during WWII. This article can at least let more people know more about what happened in China, especially my hometown, during WWII. This story's not bad and probably a great article to respect those Chinese who fought for freedom.

I once read a history book about my high school and found that the old main building of my high school was used as a Japanese military police headquarter during WWII. Many people who defied Japanese were put in custody at the basement of the main building, where exactly my classroom was.
This is why history needs to be retold even in fictional stories otherwise we may not even know that we are so close to history.

In a word, this is a good story that integrates the history of Unit 731 seamlessly.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: This is SERIOUS considering about WWⅡ which caused over 17,000,000 casualties in my home country and this is not as easy as godzilla. This is not a joke even within the original idea of the writer, no matter how you think. go back with the godzilla yet wish you have a great day with Unit 731.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's nothing wrong creating a literary piece with real historical background

If they distorted fact/history on purpose or something like that then yeah, you can criticize whatever
But they didn't, and it's a well written fictional work

Just like what Carnap claiming, let's give ppl the freedom of expression as long as they define the expression properly
Let's give ppl the freedom of literary creation as long as they writing them in a proper way
They're containing Godzilla right? I mean it's got to be Godzilla.

item.title: Absolutely Loved this SCP
item.content:encoded: SCP-4007 is probably my favorite SCP. I really enjoyed the historical context, and the inclusion of Unit 731.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your good scip. I have translated this to Japanese. But I stumbled upon several points.

  • Footnote 3: Shinka simply means "evolution", "apotheosis" is too overstating. Most Japanese think of evolution of organisms hearing "shinka". If we express apotheosis with this word, we say "Kami he no Shinka (evolution into godkinds)".
  • Footnote 5: Kenpeitai was just same as military police of other nations. Of course if you want, there might be some secret divisions in it. But whole that organization was normal.
  • File name: Wow you named first picture "Pingfang-6.jpg". What do you imply with it? (not indication, I think it is intentional)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As another Chinese, I would remind you that there are plenty of fictional stories based on those atrocities in WWII. I don't need to list them here - you must have heard of a lot of those movies, TV series, etc. in China. What I want to show as an example is SCP-CN-306, which is also based on Unit 731 and written by a Chinese. Dislike it as you may, but this at least proves that not "only Japanese will write such a document".

Personally I don't see why people cannot write fictions that are based on tragic, historical event. As long as the story reminds people of what truely happened, it is totally okay for me.

I posted the Chinese version translated by ashausesall. When I was typing in Shirō Ishii - yes, just as you, disgust and hatred of this name nearly overwhelmed me. But then I saw the introdution of Unit 731, about what they had done and how evil they were in the real history, I began to appreciate the skip. Well.. I don't think who write this down is malicious towards China as you suggested. On the contrary, I would say the author wants the readers to know and remember this war crime.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

The idea is that -4 selectively excises memories of itself such that the Foundation has no idea it was involved-in that case, in the perceived absence of other motivating factors, the Foundation believed that it had simply carried those actions out because they wanted to. Cognitive dissonance is a powerful thing.

Glad you enjoyed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this, especially because for once I was able to see a pattern instead of having it explained to me. I have to ask, though, how did -4 manage to convince the Foundation to make all those allowances/accept those results of missions?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point, and thank you. Necessary references have been changed (Thanks PeppersGhost!), as has the map image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a great read but you should probably change all references to the number 4000 to 4007 since that is this SCP's designation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I noticed a few peculiarities when I read through the list of instances, however nothing quite prepared me for that moment when I opened the bottom collapsible and saw I wasn't even half way done.

HOW DEEP DOES THIS GO?

click here for how deep (spoilers)
– hide block

To the bottom of the damn ocean is how deep.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kinda hoped that 4000-5 would be 953.

Nevertheless, I thoroughly enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ultimately, this was a very well written SCP. You definitely did your homework, and all of your references were in good taste. I enjoyed your handling of SCP-4000-4's memetic properties, and I loved the creeping buildup as I slowly figured out why killing off three of the five subjects was deemed "acceptable". Also, while slightly unconventional, I enjoyed the layout you used.

I only have two major gripes with it, and they are somewhat related.

First, I didn't like how a lot of the notes and logs flowed. They read less like how a scientifically literate special investigator would actually write and more like how a dramatic fiction author would depict the inner thoughts of their teenage main character. There were a few places that read like that, but most of my issues are exemplified by the Testimony Concerning SCP-4000-4 given by Dr. Domingo. The sheer amount of rhetorical questions used here makes Dr. Domingo come off as an overdramatic and borderline unprofessional character instead of a special investigator hand-picked by the O5 to deal with a severe lapse of information on a Keter SCP.

Second, I feel like the twist that SCP-4000-4 was memetic was spoiled by the fact that O5-3 practically spoon-fed it to me with their note in SCP-4000 Archival Notice 1. I had hardly enough time to process what the old SCP file from 1945 even meant before O5-3 was there to explain it all to me. It kind of ruined the build up that the whole article had led to because instead of giving me time to put the pieces together myself and understand the implications behind what SCP-4000-4 can actually do, O5-3 just up and blurts it out. It kind of kills the suspense and creeping dread for me, despite the whole article building those feelings up so well.

Overall, no vote from me, but only because I already have read an SCP-4000 entry that I would like to win, and also because this article is too damn good for a -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You lose absolutely nothing by being respectful of those who take issue with a piece of work that involves real-life events they feel a great deal of grief over.

This doesn't really mean that the writer should erase their work, but it does provide them an opportunity to learn, and grow, from understanding the way it affects some.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to see a comic where Electro, Luke Cage, Purple Man, Professor Xavier, and Mystique work together to deal with and contain an unspeakably strong horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't usually like articles that end without telling you what's contained, but I loved this one. Excellently done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well done. I hope the story and investigation continues in future skips. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was neat.

I really enjoyed getting a look at each individual instance of your SCP-4000, and how they were managed to be killed. In fact, those files really were what made the article for me. As such, when I got to the end of 4000-5's entry, I was wary to continue, since I felt you had reached a good stopping point. That the mission could not be completed since 4000-5 would be literally impossible to find.

But hey, the twist about 4000-4's ability and the elaborate ruse pulled on the Foundation paid off, in my opinion. Whatever is in that pentagram and sank that fleet is quite an interesting mystery, one that I was really hoping to see concluded. Guess the mystery is part of the fun of this article, in the end. I can understand the reasoning behind leaving that a loose end.

All in all a really solid article for the contest. +1 wei.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers
No more!

I think a lot of people are reading this and interpreting it as if the Foundation is giving up. It's not that they're giving up per se, but that they're recognizing that this may be a problem they simply won't ever know the answer to. There's simply been too much time passed, and too little recorded that they may not ever have the full picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The X-Men comics have used the concentration camps as a background for more than fifty years now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really loved this all the way up to the last four paragraphs, which bugged me almost enough not to give a +1. I don't think it follows from the rest of the story. Some things should stay unknowable? This is clearly something that's going to arise and the Foundation should want to know very much about it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, I’m glad you enjoyed the article! I’m very happy to see that it left such an impact on you.

+ show block
– hide block

On the subject of revised containment procedures, in this case, the justification is that personnel below Level 4 don’t know about the revised procedures at all. For most personnel, nothing has changed with regards to Pingfang 5 containment; it’s only on the higher levels that people have active knowledge of the change. It’s easier this way to avoid someone slipping to 4000-4 that they know his secret, which could immediately invalidate years and years of research.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Wow you are a very gifted techincal writer, I will be collecting numerous sentences and phrases from here. This article had the best mystery-upheaval of all the entries when the pentagram is introduced and -4 starts to be questioned. I almost said something like “Oooh shit!” out loud. The pacing is so effective and you make what could have been a dry subject a very lush article. I get a really great Metal Gear villain vibe from the Pingfang 5. Definitely a +1.

My only criticism is really a pet peeve that happens all the time, is present in several of the contest’s entries, and really isn’t a terrible thing: the complete rewriting of the containment procedures by the end. My opinion is that if these are now the real containment procedures, the author (both in-universe and out) should put them as the actual containment procedures, barring some well-justified security clearance. The digging someone in-universe would have to do to get the real ones is inexcusably inefficient and would contribute to the fumbling of important data, the likes of which put the Foundation at such a knowledge-deficit in this case.

In conclusion I love this article. Great work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really, really well-written, and for that, you get an emphatic upvote. But I must say that what I'm really looking forward to is r/SCPDeclassified to do a breakdown of it, because I'm definitely not clever enough to piece together the whole story myself. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, as a national of the victims of war, I will never write a story about "731 troops" in this way.

You won't write a story? then don't write it. Just because this story hurts your feeling doesn't mean it can't be written or read. So you're a national of a victimised nation back in the War? Cool, but so? Not everyone here are from a victimised nation, or like to identify themselves as such. My homeland was occupied by Japan 70 years ago, and then by the Communist since 1997, but did I find any fictions involving Japan or the GRU-P any less enjoyable? No, because the war has ended for like, what, 70+ years already for God sake. Also, I really hate it when people go about flashing that label around and restrict a writer's freedom of ideas.

As for Auschwitz, I'm pretty sure that it was used as the background element for several stories, COLD HARPER comes to mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He was probably cutting the other parts tl add them to himself, since after -01 dies -02 also dies of eletric shock and after -02 dies -03 dies after firing multiple shots to something that survived those shots

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding Mr Tidal, but with slightly different reasons.

I do want to read some article about Auschwitz (unfortunately I'm not German nor Jewish though), but only high quality ones, which match the depth and complexity of feeling it gives. I'm not a fan of SCP-1145, but it's not because I'm descended from those who got indirect atomic exposure. It's rather because it's just a radioactive teddy bear that chases you, lacking any depth beyond that. It's really nothing to do with Nagasaki. Similarly, at this point, Unit 731 in this article looks almost replaceable to me with any group related to Imperial Japan and its allies. Not worth a frown it causes.

Plus, Unit 731 is heavily focused on conventional medicine and BC weapons, and it doesn't feel fitted to go into anomalous enhancement of soldiers nor any esoteric containment effort.

So I'd suggest creating a totally new fictional troop dedicated for anomalous research, or, maybe, shinto-craze religious-sounding body, as an alternative. Human experimentation was not monopolized by Unit 731; while 731 facilities were a hub of experiments, other more obscure groups like Unit 516 existed, and in 1989, human bones worth of more than hundred people were discovered at the site once the army surgeon school stood in Tokyo. Their limbs were mostly severed into some parts, and some skulls had gunshot wounds and sword cuts.

(And the JP branch has Unit Negative Numbers, which was dedicated for one PoI's ambition for immortality.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are correct, this is a mistake. Working to fix it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was kinda hung up on the ending, got a little unsatisfied. After realizing the symbolism though (as I said above), I started to like it. I might be overthinking, but damn this shit is deep.

Tentative upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One thing I noticed: the diagram in the special report alpha has 4000-2 in Guiyuang and 4000-3 in the Philippines, but the pages state that -2 was the one found in the Philippines and -3 in Guiyuang.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, I have to clarify and say that I am not, in fact, Japanese, or of Japanese descent. Contrary to your point, I am actually of Chinese descent, the primary country affected by Unit 731 in real life.

However, I understand your viewpoint. It is often difficult to fictionalize historical events in a way that doesn't trivialize or otherwise reduce the importance that said events had on real life. Too often, when we simply use these events as a backdrop for other stories, we forget that they actually happened to very real people who simply had to deal with this as their reality. This is doubly important when said events were as horrifying or senselessly cruel as what Unit 731 did.

So then, how to write about these kinds of tragedies in a fictionalized way? Well, the important thing in my mind is to not create some sort of historical revisionist narrative that takes away from what they were. It was a genocide, it was a massacre, it was a war crime, in this article and in real life. Reading about these events should make you feel uncomfortable. It should make you take a step back. Because if nothing else, it's a gutpunch to remember that this all really did happen.

I think that writing about these events gives us an important opportunity to remember them. Many people I talk to know little about Unit 731, the Tuskegee Experiments, Soviet pogroms, the Holocaust, and more events perpetuated by every nation during that dark time. Writing about them (even in a fictionalized way) is a way to bring them back to our memory. As HumidHermit said, there are things that have been done, and things that can't be forgotten. In this case, forgetting them and not writing about them would be a disservice to the people who lived that history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If a German uses the Auschwitz concentration camp as a background to write an SCP, what do you think? I don't think you will deny that SCP is a horror story based on entertainment.

First, like ARD said, there would be nothing wrong with it, if the article doesn't see the context of war as "entertainment". Second, I don't think you get the main idea of the article.

What the author has written here is not "hatred and anger against the Japanese", but about the symbolism of how the pain of war is still there. Maybe I'm interpreting this wrong, but I see the fact that SCP-4000-4, the one that uses anti-meme to conceal himself, is alive to fight represents the lingering grief. He can't let the memory of war go, which is ironic of his power — erasing memories. This invisible monster that he is fighting, the one that sunk so many Japanese ships, symbolizes that in war, anyone has to suffer; not just the countries damaged by Japanese troops, but also the troops themselves. Loss is unidentifiable and unforgiving here.

I understand that you might be offended when you see a conflicting representation of history. Same with me too. My country was considered evil just because we were communists, but in reality we are the victims.

Nonetheless, there are things that have been done, things that can't be forgotten.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So why don't you answer my question positively: If a German uses the Auschwitz concentration camp as a background to write an SCP, what do you think?
I don't think you will deny that SCP is a horror story based on entertainment.

A similar incident: A woman was condemned for publishing a photo of a smile taken at the Auschwitz concentration camp.
why?

Personally, as a national of the victims of war, I will never write a story about "731 troops" in this way.
It is also difficult for me to imagine that people from countries with 731 injuries will write this story.
Probably only Japanese, will write such a document.
If this document has any effect on me, it is hatred and anger against the Japanese.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see what the issue is? If the article trivializes the atrocities of Unit 731 or Imperial Japan then it would indeed be cause for criticism, but there's nothing inherently wrong with incorporating them into the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If a German writes an SCP with the Auschwitz concentration camp as a story background, what do you think?
I will be very disgusted, just like disgusting with this document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a sucker for a good mystery story, and this does it nicely.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic, absolutely superb. It's entirely daring that you only leave us with questions by the end, and I don't know how you managed to keep that from being frustrating, as other articles have done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconded. I loved the beginning, middle and everything up to the revised containment procedures. Then the story and my enjoyment of it just stopped. It wasn't a bad ending per se, it just was incredibly sudden and left way too many loose ends for me. (Why was -4 cutting out the anomalous parts of the other instances? Why was he driven to kill them? How does -5 tie into it? Why are the Foundation no longer hunting -5?) Stuff like this, in addition to the questions already posed in the article itself are what is keeping me from fully upvoting the article.

Although, I am happy this didn't become a Godzilla thing, I was actually quite scared that would be what it would become after I read some of it.

Tentative upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This easily became one of my favorite entries and I'm glad you got around to posting. I was getting worried :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was one of my more enjoyed 4000 entries - the historical detail, pacing and buildup are all on point - until I got to the ending, which was disappointing and seems to boil down to little more than 'something big and spooky is going on'. Maybe my imagination has become stunted, but I'm not a fan of these entries that leave the ending completely hanging at the best of times (it seems to be a disagreeable pattern amongst a lot of the entries) and the coy nod to 'inherently unknowable truths' doesn't help. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this in the draft stage and I like it here, good work! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed, thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. Interesting approach to history.

Note: On the tabs for individual members, 4000-2 appears twice.

item.title: Author Overview
item.content:encoded:
I fear all we have done is to awaken a sleeping giant and fill him with a terrible resolve.

- Isoroku Yamamoto, Tora! Tora! Tora! (1980)

It's good to be back.

This is the longest work that I've ever published for this site, and definitely the most ambitious. I hope you enjoy reading it as much as I did writing it.

I must thank a phenomenal cast of reviewers who greatly assisted me in this process, whether it was providing detailed advice, or simply reading it when I asked.

  • Many thanks to Riemann for his initial reviewing of the earliest stages of my draft, and for providing a wonderful voice into writing style.
  • A Random Day was critical for refining this into the version that you see now, as well as giving me the inspiration for the title.
  • Modern_Erasmus was very helpful for his insight into the subtler points of the draft, which helped me guide my overall vision.
  • Additionally, the lovely reviewers of the SCPD Discord were great readers whenever I bugged them.
+ Stray Observations
- Stray Observations
  • Kagemusha - Literally translated, this is "Shadow Warrior," but in Japanese, it's used as the term for a political decoy. More abstractly, it refers to a 1980 film by Akira Kurosawa that centered around the concept.
  • Unit 731 was, in fact, a terrifyingly real institution that did in fact commit all of the war crimes that I used as a background for the Pingfang 5. Learn more here!
  • A strong basis for this skip was my interest in WW2 Japanese holdouts, who were a very real phenomenon whereby numerous Japanese soldiers refused to stand down following the war. Learn more here!
+ Image Attributions
- Image Attributions

Image 1, the Pingfang 5 itself, is from this Flickr collection, which is CC compliant.

Image 2, the IJAMEA ensign, is from the IJAMEA Hub, on the site itself.

Image 3, of Director Shiro Ishii, is from Wikimedia Commons, and is public domain.

Image 4, SCP-4000-1, is from This history website, and was labeled for reuse.

Image 5, SCP-4000-2, is from Wikimedia Commons, and is public domain. Fun fact: the image is Shoichi Yokoi, a real life Japanese holdout!

Image 6, SCP-4000-3, is from Wikimedia Commons as well.

Image 7, SCP-4000-4, is from this WW2 photo website, and was labeled for reuse.

Image 8, the map of the Southeast Asian Ocean, is public domain as well, and then modified by myself.

Image 9, the divers, is a photo from the US National Park Service website, and is public domain.
